subject: Pathology
definition: Muscle Disorders: Pathology of the musculoskeletal and neuromuscular systems affecting skeletal muscles, motor unit function, and transmission at the neuromuscular junction. These encompass inherited dystrophies, acquired inflammatory and metabolic myopathies, transmission defects, and neurogenic and disuse atrophies, which alter muscle mechanics and require precise, fatigue-paced physical therapy.
principle: Duchenne & Becker Muscular Dystrophies (DMD vs. BMD): X-linked recessive degenerative muscle disorders characterized by progressive muscle weakness. 1) DMD: Caused by a complete absence of the structural sarcolemmal protein dystrophin, which normally anchors the internal actin cytoskeleton to the extracellular matrix. Without dystrophin, muscle contraction induces mechanical sarcolemmal tearing, calcium influx, myofiber necrosis, and eventual replacement by fatty and fibrous tissue (causing calf pseudohypertrophy). Clinically presents in young boys with Gowers sign, progressive pelvic-girdle weakness, cardiomyopathy, and respiratory failure. 2) BMD: Caused by a mutation resulting in a truncated, partially functional dystrophin protein. BMD has a much later onset, milder progression, and normal life expectancy, compared to the severe early-childhood progression of DMD.

Myasthenia Gravis (MG) & Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome (LEMS): Neuromuscular junction transmission disorders causing fluctuating weakness. 1) MG: An autoimmune type II hypersensitivity disorder characterized by IgG autoantibodies targeting postsynaptic nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (AChR) or muscle-specific receptor tyrosine kinase (MuSK). This leads to receptor internalization, degradation, and a flattened postsynaptic membrane. Clinically, it presents with fluctuating skeletal muscle weakness that worsens with repetitive exertion and improves with rest (diurnal fatigability, ptosis, diplopia). Severe respiratory muscle involvement can precipitate a life-threatening myasthenic crisis. 2) LEMS: An autoimmune paraneoplastic disorder (strongly associated with small-cell lung carcinoma) characterized by antibodies against presynaptic voltage-gated calcium channels (VGCC), preventing acetylcholine release. Unlike MG, muscle strength in LEMS temporarily improves with repetitive stimulation (incremental response).

Inflammatory Myopathies - Polymyositis (PM) & Dermatomyositis (DM): Systemic, immunologically mediated inflammatory muscle diseases. 1) Polymyositis: Characterized by CD8+ cytotoxic T-cell-mediated injury of muscle fibers. The inflammatory infiltrate is located in the endomysium (surrounding individual fibers), and muscle biopsy reveals variation in fiber size and necrotic fibers. It presents with symmetric, progressive proximal muscle weakness and significantly elevated serum creatine kinase (CK) levels. 2) Dermatomyositis: Characterized by CD4+ helper T-cell and B-cell-mediated microangiopathy. The inflammation is perimysial (around muscle fascicles) and perivascular, leading to perifascicular atrophy. It presents with proximal muscle weakness and distinct cutaneous signs: a heliotrope rash (violaceous erythema of the upper eyelids) and Gottron papules (scaling erythematous plaques over the knuckles and interphalangeal joints), with a strong association with systemic malignancies in older adults.

Neurogenic, Denervation, and Disuse Atrophy: Pathways leading to skeletal muscle wasting and fiber shrinkage. 1) Neurogenic and Denervation Atrophy: Caused by a loss of motor innervation (e.g., lower motor neuron lesions, peripheral nerve trauma, motor neuron disease). On biopsy, denervated muscle fibers shrink, take on an angulated shape, and undergo 'group atrophy' as adjacent surviving motor units attempt to reinnervate them (reinnervation changes the fiber type to match the new motor unit). Electromyography (EMG) reveals denervation potentials (fibrillations and positive sharp waves), and patients often display visible muscle fasciculations. 2) Disuse Atrophy: Occurs secondary to prolonged immobilization, plaster casting, bed rest, or joint pain. There is a reduction in muscle fiber volume without any loss of nerve supply. It selectively affects Type I (slow-twitch oxidative, postural) muscle fibers. Unlike denervation atrophy, disuse atrophy is highly reversible with progressive weight-bearing and resistive physical rehabilitation.

Rhabdomyolysis & Metabolic Myopathies: Muscle breakdown and metabolic storage diseases. 1) Rhabdomyolysis: Severe, acute skeletal muscle breakdown resulting in the rapid release of intracellular components (myoglobin, creatine kinase, and potassium) into the systemic circulation. It can be triggered by crush injuries, severe trauma, extreme overexertion, or heat stroke. The pathomechanism involves cell membrane disruption, massive intracellular calcium influx, and cell death. Clinically characterized by a triad of severe muscle pain, profound muscle weakness, and dark tea-colored urine (myoglobinuria). It poses a critical, life-threatening risk of Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) due to direct myoglobin toxicity and tubular casting in the kidneys. 2) Metabolic Myopathies: Hereditary disorders of muscle carbohydrate or lipid metabolism, such as McArdle disease (myophosphorylase deficiency, glycogen storage disease type V). These present with exercise intolerance, muscle cramping, and transient post-exercise myoglobinuria, failing to produce a normal rise in blood lactate during ischemic forearm exercise.

Muscular Regeneration & Fibrofatty Replacement: Healing mechanisms and structural failure of muscle tissue. In response to minor trauma or localized focal injury, skeletal muscle possesses a high regenerative capacity driven by satellite cells. These are quiescent, undifferentiated myogenic stem cells residing between the basal lamina and sarcolemma. Following injury, satellite cells proliferate, differentiate into myoblasts, and fuse to form multinucleated regenerates. However, when muscle injury is severe, repetitive, or chronic (such as in advanced Duchenne muscular dystrophy or following extreme muscle tears), the regenerative pool becomes exhausted. Chronic necrotic areas undergo fibrovascular repair, leading to extensive endomysial fibrosis and adipose tissue infiltration, permanently compromising muscle contraction velocity, excursion, and active tension generation.
clinicalPearls: DMD Eccentric Strength Contraindication: In patients with Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy, eccentric muscle contractions (where muscle lengthens under tension, e.g., descending stairs or resisted manual lengthening) are strictly contraindicated. The lack of functional dystrophin makes the sarcolemma fragile, and eccentric loading causes rapid, irreversible mechanical tearing of muscle fibers and accelerates disease progression.

Myasthenia Gravis Pacing and Fatigue Management: Physical therapy for Myasthenia Gravis must focus on low-to-moderate intensity aerobic and submaximal strength training. Exercises should be performed in the morning (when neuromuscular transmission is most efficient) or shortly after taking anticholinesterase medication (e.g., Pyridostigmine). The therapist must immediately stop training if facial ptosis, dyspnea, or severe proximal fatigue occurs, to avoid precipitating a life-threatening myasthenic crisis.

Rhabdomyolysis Acute Stage Absolute Exercise Contraindication: During active rhabdomyolysis (manifested by tea-colored urine, extreme muscle soreness, and massive elevations of serum creatine kinase), any exercise or muscle loading is strictly and absolutely contraindicated. The therapist must refer the patient to emergency care for immediate intravenous hydration and renal monitoring to prevent acute renal failure.

Polymyositis Active Flare Exercise Limits: During acute inflammatory flares of Polymyositis or Dermatomyositis (indicated by sharp rises in CK levels and extreme muscle tenderness), active resistance training is contraindicated to prevent exacerbating muscle fiber necrosis. Rehabilitation during this phase must be limited to gentle, pain-free active-assisted or passive range of motion exercises to prevent joint contractures.

Neurogenic Atrophy Submaximal Pacing: When rehabilitating patients with denervation or neurogenic muscle atrophy (e.g., peripheral nerve injuries, poliomyelitis survivors), aggressive resistive exercise of the partially denervated muscle can cause 'overwork weakness' due to metabolic exhaustion of surviving motor units. Therapy should focus on low-resistance, high-repetition exercises and orthotic support (such as an AFO for foot drop).

Disuse Atrophy Target Recovery: Disuse atrophy primarily targets Type I (slow-twitch) postural fibers. Rehabilitation must utilize early mobilization, progressive closed-kinetic chain exercises, and low-load, high-volume resistance training to selectively restore oxidative capacity, capillary density, and postural endurance.
vivaQuestions: Question: Describe the biochemical and structural pathogenesis of Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD) and contrast it with Becker Muscular Dystrophy (BMD). | Answer: DMD is caused by an X-linked recessive frameshift mutation leading to the complete absence of the structural protein dystrophin, which normally connects the internal F-actin cytoskeleton to the extracellular dystroglycan-sarcoglycan complex. Without dystrophin, normal muscle contraction causes mechanical sarcolemmal shearing, calcium influx, myofiber necrosis, and fibrofatty replacement. BMD is caused by a non-frameshift mutation producing a truncated, partially functional dystrophin, resulting in a much later onset, milder proximal weakness, and a near-normal lifespan. | Examiner Key Points: Absence of dystrophin, mechanical sarcolemmal tearing, calcium influx, myofiber necrosis, fibrofatty replacement, frameshift vs. non-frameshift. | Clinical Importance: Informs the clinician that eccentric strengthening is strictly contraindicated in DMD as it accelerates physical sarcolemmal destruction.

Question: Explain the pathomechanism of Myasthenia Gravis (MG) and contrast it with Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome (LEMS). | Answer: Myasthenia Gravis is a post-synaptic autoimmune disorder where IgG autoantibodies block, internalize, and destroy nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (AChR) or MuSK at the motor endplate, causing fluctuating weakness that worsens with repetition (decremental response on EMG). LEMS is a pre-synaptic paraneoplastic disorder (usually associated with small-cell lung cancer) where autoantibodies target voltage-gated calcium channels (VGCC), blocking calcium-dependent acetylcholine vesicle release. Muscle strength in LEMS temporarily improves with repetitive contraction (incremental response) because calcium accumulates in the terminal with rapid stimulation. | Examiner Key Points: Postsynaptic AChR vs. Presynaptic VGCC, decremental vs. incremental EMG response, association of LEMS with small-cell lung cancer, fatiguability vs. post-activation facilitation. | Clinical Importance: Guides the therapist on exercise pacing; MG requires frequent rest to recover postsynaptic transmission, whereas LEMS may show transient improvement in strength after brief warm-up contractions.

Question: Differentiate between Polymyositis and Dermatomyositis based on their immunological pathology and clinical signs. | Answer: Polymyositis is a cell-mediated autoimmune disease where CD8+ cytotoxic T-cells directly invade muscle fibers. The inflammatory infiltrate is endomysial (within the fascicle surrounding individual fibers). Dermatomyositis is a humoral-mediated autoimmune disease where CD4+ helper T-cells and B-cells cause a microangiopathy of perimysial capillaries, leading to perifascicular atrophy. Clinically, both present with proximal muscle weakness, but Dermatomyositis is characterized by distinct cutaneous rashes: a heliotrope rash on the eyelids, Gottron papules on the knuckles, and has a strong association with underlying internal malignancies in older adults. | Examiner Key Points: CD8+ cytotoxic T-cells (endomysial) vs CD4+/B-cells (perimysial/microangiopathy), perifascicular atrophy, heliotrope rash, Gottron papules, malignancy risk. | Clinical Importance: Alerts the physical therapist to monitor older DM patients for red flags of systemic cancer and strictly limit exercise during acute inflammatory flares.

Question: What is Rhabdomyolysis, why is it a medical emergency, and what clinical signs must a physical therapist look for? | Answer: Rhabdomyolysis is the acute, massive breakdown of skeletal muscle fibers, releasing intracellular contents (myoglobin, CK, and potassium) into the bloodstream. It is a medical emergency because circulating myoglobin is filtered by the kidneys, where it precipitates in the renal tubules, causing acute tubular necrosis and life-threatening Acute Kidney Injury (AKI), exacerbated by hyperkalemia-induced cardiac arrhythmias. The key clinical signs are severe, generalized muscle pain and stiffness, profound weakness, and dark tea- or cola-colored urine (myoglobinuria) following intense overexertion or trauma. | Examiner Key Points: Muscle fiber breakdown, myoglobin release, acute kidney injury (AKI), tea-colored urine, hyperkalemia, medical emergency. | Clinical Importance: Mandates immediate suspension of all physical activities and emergency referral for aggressive IV hydration and medical stabilization.

Question: How do you pathologically and electrophysiologically differentiate neurogenic muscle atrophy from myopathic muscle disorders? | Answer: Neurogenic muscle atrophy is caused by motor neuron or peripheral nerve lesions, presenting histologically as clusters of shrunken, angulated muscle fibers (group atrophy) with EMG findings of denervation potentials (fibrillations, positive sharp waves) and giant motor unit potentials from reinnervation. Myopathic disorders (primary muscle diseases like muscular dystrophy or polymyositis) present histologically with random variation in fiber size, central nuclei, degenerating/regenerating fibers, and fibrofatty replacement, with EMG findings of small-amplitude, short-duration, polyphasic motor unit potentials during voluntary contraction. | Examiner Key Points: Shrunken/angulated fibers, group atrophy, denervation potentials vs. random size variation, central nuclei, small/short polyphasic EMG potentials. | Clinical Importance: Helps the physiotherapist distinguish nerve-related weakness (requiring orthotics and nerve regeneration pacing) from muscle-intrinsic disease (requiring fatigue management and metabolic pacing).

Question: Define Disuse Atrophy, explain its pathophysiology, and state which muscle fiber types are primarily affected compared to denervation atrophy. | Answer: Disuse Atrophy is the reduction in muscle fiber volume and protein content due to prolonged lack of mechanical loading, joint immobilization, or bed rest, without any disruption of nerve supply. It is driven by accelerated protein degradation via the ubiquitin-proteasome pathway. It selectively and primarily affects Type I (slow-twitch oxidative, postural) muscle fibers. In contrast, denervation atrophy is caused by direct loss of nerve supply, affects both Type I and Type II fibers in a group distribution, and is accompanied by denervation potentials on EMG. Disuse atrophy is highly reversible with progressive physical loading. | Examiner Key Points: Lack of mechanical loading, ubiquitin-proteasome pathway, selective Type I fiber atrophy, intact nerve supply vs. lost nerve supply (denervation). | Clinical Importance: Guides the therapist to prioritize low-load, high-repetition, closed-kinetic chain exercises and progressive weight-bearing to specifically target and rebuild Type I slow-twitch postural stabilizer fibers.

Question: Explain the role of satellite cells in muscular regeneration and describe what occurs pathologically when their regenerative capacity is overwhelmed. | Answer: Satellite cells are quiescent, undifferentiated myogenic stem cells located between the sarcolemma and the muscle basal lamina. Following focal muscle injury or microtrauma, they become activated, proliferate, differentiate into myoblasts, and fuse to form new multinucleated myofibers or repair damaged ones (indicated by central nuclei on histopathology). In chronic, severe, or repetitive muscle injuries (such as advanced DMD), the satellite cell pool becomes depleted and exhausted. Healing then occurs via fibrovascular tissue repair, resulting in extensive endomysial fibrosis and adipose tissue infiltration (fibrofatty replacement), which permanently limits muscle contractility and increases muscle stiffness. | Examiner Key Points: Satellite cells, quiescent stem cells, central nuclei, cellular depletion/exhaustion, fibrofatty replacement, permanent contractility loss. | Clinical Importance: Underlines why aggressive, repetitive overworking of dystrophic muscles leads to permanent loss of muscle tissue, requiring therapists to strictly adhere to submaximal, non-fatiguing exercises.

Question: What is Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome (LEMS), and how do you clinically differentiate it from Myasthenia Gravis (MG) during physical evaluation? | Answer: LEMS is an autoimmune presynaptic disorder characterized by antibodies against voltage-gated calcium channels, leading to reduced acetylcholine release. It is strongly associated with small-cell lung carcinoma. Clinically, it can be differentiated from MG because LEMS presents with proximal lower-limb weakness that temporarily improves with repetitive voluntary contraction (post-activation facilitation or incremental response on EMG), and deep tendon reflexes are hypoactive or absent but return after contraction. MG presents with ocular/bulbar and proximal upper-extremity weakness that worsens with repetition (decremental response), and tendon reflexes are normal. | Examiner Key Points: Presynaptic calcium channels, small-cell lung carcinoma, lower-limb weakness, post-activation facilitation (improvement with repetition), hyper/hyporeflexia. | Clinical Importance: Ensures the physical therapist correctly identifies LEMS as a potential paraneoplastic red flag (requiring systemic cancer screening) and utilizes brief isometric contractions to temporarily facilitate muscle activation before training.

mcqs.question: A complete absence of the structural sarcolemmal-anchoring protein dystrophin is the underlying genetic etiology of:
mcqs.explanation: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD) is caused by an X-linked recessive frameshift mutation resulting in a total absence of dystrophin. Becker Muscular Dystrophy (BMD) involves a partially functional, truncated dystrophin.

mcqs.question: Myasthenia Gravis is an autoimmune disorder primarily characterized by autoantibodies directed against which of the following targets?
options: Myelin sheath proteins in peripheral nerves
mcqs.explanation: Myasthenia Gravis is a type II hypersensitivity reaction where IgG autoantibodies target postsynaptic nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (AChR) or MuSK, impairing neuromuscular transmission.

mcqs.question: Which of the following clinical and laboratory findings is a critical red flag indicating the development of acute Rhabdomyolysis?
options: Severe joint crepitus and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR)
Fluctuating ptosis, double vision, and normal serum creatine kinase (CK)
Symmetric proximal weakness, heliotrope rash, and low serum potassium
Severe muscle pain, dark tea-colored urine, and massive elevation of serum CK
mcqs.answer: Severe muscle pain, dark tea-colored urine, and massive elevation of serum CK
mcqs.explanation: The clinical triad of rhabdomyolysis is severe muscle pain, weakness, and dark tea-colored urine (due to myoglobinuria), accompanied by extremely high serum CK levels, posing a major risk for acute kidney injury.

mcqs.question: An autoimmune neuromuscular disorder associated with small-cell lung carcinoma, where antibodies block presynaptic voltage-gated calcium channels, is:
mcqs.explanation: Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome (LEMS) is a paraneoplastic disorder caused by autoantibodies against presynaptic voltage-gated calcium channels, strongly associated with small-cell lung carcinoma.

mcqs.question: A muscle biopsy showing endomysial inflammatory infiltrates consisting primarily of cytotoxic CD8+ T-cells invading healthy, non-necrotic muscle fibers is characteristic of:
mcqs.explanation: Polymyositis is a cell-mediated autoimmune myopathy characterized by CD8+ T-cell infiltration located in the endomysium. Dermatomyositis features CD4+ helper T-cell and B-cell perimysial microangiopathy.

mcqs.question: A 45-year-old female presents with progressive proximal muscle weakness, a purple-violet heliotrope rash on her upper eyelids, and scaling erythematous Gottron papules over her knuckles. What is the most likely diagnosis?
mcqs.explanation: Dermatomyositis is characterized by proximal muscle weakness accompanied by classic skin lesions: a heliotrope rash (violaceous eyelid erythema) and Gottron papules (scaling knuckle lesions).

mcqs.question: Biopsy of a muscle following a peripheral nerve laceration reveals groups of small, shrunken, angulated muscle fibers surrounded by normal fibers. This histological feature is known as:
mcqs.explanation: Loss of innervation (neurogenic atrophy) leads to individual fiber shrinkage (angulated fibers) and 'group atrophy' as adjacent surviving motor axons attempt to reinnervate the denervated fibers.

mcqs.question: Which muscle fiber type is selectively and primarily lost/atrophied in patients undergoing prolonged bed rest or joint immobilization (Disuse Atrophy)?
options: All muscle fiber types are atrophied symmetrically
mcqs.explanation: Disuse atrophy selectively and preferentially targets Type I slow-twitch postural muscle fibers due to the loss of continuous tonic anti-gravity activation, though it is highly reversible with rehabilitation.

reference: Textbook of Pathology by Harsh Mohan
